hero.subtitle: The 5th ISTSDC & The XXIX National Symposium.
hero.theme: "Advancing Sustainable Mobility Solutions for Resilient and Inclusive Future"

about.label: About
about.title: What is
about.description: Forum Studi Transportasi antar Perguruan Tinggi (FSTPT) is a scientific forum established to foster interaction and communication among students, lecturers, and researchers from universities and institutions across Indonesia engaged in education, research, and community service in the transportation sector. FSTPT is realized through: (1) a mid-year national transportation seminar, (2) an end-of-year national transportation symposium, and (3) the International Symposium on Transportation and Sustainable Development Conference (ISTSDC), held biennially. These platforms serve as avenues for exchanging research findings, scientific studies, and professional experiences to strengthen the Tridharma of Higher Education and support national transportation research and innovation programs. Since its inception in 1998, the end-of-year symposium alone has featured nearly 6,000 presentations by students and lecturers in transportation sciences, while ISTSDC further expands international collaboration and knowledge exchange in sustainable transport development.
about.withTheme: with the theme "Advancing Sustainable Mobility Solutions for Resilient and Inclusive Future"

objectives.title: OBJECTIVES
objectives.subtitle: This symposium is designed with three main, mutually supportive objectives to advance transportation research in Indonesia.

items.title: Sustainable & Resilient Solutions
items.desc: Formulate sustainable, resilient, and inclusive transportation solutions that address the geographical challenges of West Sumatra—mountainous terrain and disaster-prone areas—ensuring safety, accessibility, and long-term sustainability.

items.title: Innovation in Infrastructure
items.desc: Promote innovation in road planning and multimodal infrastructure that adapts to difficult terrain while supporting energy efficiency, digitalization, and the adoption of environmentally friendly technologies.

items.title: Strengthening Collaboration
items.desc: Strengthen collaboration among academics, practitioners, and policymakers to design resilient transportation systems and produce technical recommendations and policy insights relevant to sustainable mobility development in Indonesia and the Southeast Asian region.

activities.title: MAIN ACTIVITIES
activities.subtitle: A series of activities designed to provide the best academic and professional experience for all participants.
activities.block1Desc: The Vth International Symposium on Transportation, Sustainable Development, and Civil Engineering (ISTSDC) combined with the XXIXth National Symposium of FSTPT will feature a series of international and national symposium sessions, including plenary and parallel discussions. These sessions will present keynote speeches and research papers under the theme "Advancing Sustainable Mobility Solutions for Resilient and Inclusive Future", covering 19 sub-themes such as sustainable transport policy, digitalization, road safety, inclusive public transport, green vehicle innovation, and resilient multimodal infrastructure.
activities.block2Desc: In addition to the symposium, the Annual Members' Meeting (Rapat Anggota Akhir Tahun) of FSTPT will be held as a formal gathering to evaluate yearly activities, strengthen organizational networks, and plan future collaborative initiatives. This meeting will serve as a strategic forum to align academic and professional contributions toward advancing sustainable transportation in Indonesia.
activities.block3Desc: The program will also include workshops and training sessions designed to provide hands-on knowledge and practical skills. These workshops will focus on disaster-resilient road planning, sustainable mobility innovations, and the application of digital tools for transport analysis. Through interactive learning, participants will enhance their capacity to address the challenges of disaster-prone regions and difficult terrains, particularly in West Sumatra.
activities.detailSubtitle: Advancing Sustainable Mobility Solutions for Resilient and Inclusive Future
activities.detailP1: The 5th International Symposium on Transportation, Sustainable Development, and Civil Engineering (ISTSDC) together with the 29th National Symposium of FSTPT carries the theme "Advancing Sustainable Mobility Solutions for Resilient and Inclusive Future" as a commitment of academics, practitioners, and policymakers to drive the transformation of transportation systems that are efficient, low-carbon, safe, and sustainable.
activities.detailP2: With 19 sub-themes covering policy, digitalization, road safety, inclusive public transport, green vehicle innovation, and the strengthening of land, sea, air, and railway infrastructure, this forum serves as a collaborative platform across disciplines and across nations to formulate sustainable mobility solutions.
activities.detailP3: Universitas Andalas, particularly the Department of Civil Engineering, has consistently highlighted disaster risk reduction and sustainability issues through various national and international conferences. The organization of the 5th ISTSDC combined with the 29th FSTPT Symposium is expected to further strengthen networks among academics, practitioners, and stakeholders at both national and international levels, while producing technical recommendations and policy insights relevant to the development of sustainable transportation in Indonesia and the Southeast Asian region.

subthemes.label: Theme
subthemes.title: Sub-Themes
subthemes.subtitle: 19 sub-themes covering various important aspects in sustainable transportation development in Indonesia.

items.title: Sustainable Transport Policy & Governance
items.desc: Transportation policies and governance that promote sustainability, efficiency, and equitable access for all segments of society form a crucial foundation for achieving sustainable mobility. With sustainability-oriented transport policies, every community—both urban and rural—can enjoy fair, safe, and affordable transportation access. This directly supports SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) by strengthening resilient and innovative transport infrastructure development while fostering industrial and technological growth. At the same time, inclusive transport policies contribute to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by creating livable, efficient, and environmentally friendly cities, where integrated public transport serves as the backbone of community mobility. Furthermore, sound transport governance cannot operate effectively without cross-sector and cross-national collaboration, thereby reinforcing SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals) through strategic partnerships among governments, academia, private sectors, and civil society. Thus, sustainable transport policy is not merely a regulatory instrument but a global strategy to build a resilient, inclusive, and equitable mobility system.

items.title: Evidence-Based & Participatory Mobility Planning
items.desc: Data-driven mobility planning that actively involves communities plays a crucial role in building inclusive and sustainable transportation systems. By leveraging operational data, travel patterns, and user needs, planning can deliver solutions that are more targeted, efficient, and adaptable to the dynamics of urban life. Community participation ensures that transportation policies are not solely focused on technical efficiency, but also address social aspects, accessibility, and equity. This approach aligns with SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by supporting the creation of safe, resilient, and inclusive cities, and with SDG 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions) through transparency and public participation in decision-making. Moreover, integrating data with community engagement strengthens SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals), as it fosters cross-sector collaboration among governments, academia, transport operators, and citizens. Thus, participatory and data-driven mobility is not merely a technical strategy, but a powerful instrument for sustainable development—balancing efficiency, social justice, and environmental sustainability.

items.title: Digitalization, AI & Smart Mobility Systems
items.desc: Data-driven mobility planning and public participation ensure more inclusive and targeted transportation solutions. This approach supports SDG 4 by opening access to education, SDG 11 by building sustainable cities, and SDG 17 through cross-sector collaboration. Meanwhile, the use of digital technology, IoT, and AI improves transportation efficiency and safety. This strengthens SDG 9 through industrial innovation, supports SDG 11 with efficient smart cities, and strengthens SDG 17 through the integration of global transportation systems.

items.title: Resilient Infrastructure in Disaster-Prone Regions
items.desc: Building resilient transportation infrastructure in disaster-prone areas is a crucial strategy for maintaining connectivity and the sustainability of public services during crises. Infrastructure designed with the risks of earthquakes, floods, landslides, and the impacts of climate change in mind will minimize disruptions to public mobility and logistics distribution. This approach aligns with SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) by encouraging innovation in more adaptive design and construction technologies, and supports SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by ensuring cities and communities remain connected even in the face of disasters. Furthermore, resilient infrastructure contributes to SDG 13 (Climate Action) by integrating climate mitigation and adaptation principles into transportation planning. Therefore, building resilient transportation infrastructure is not only a physical investment but also a policy instrument for strengthening social, economic, and environmental resilience in disaster-prone areas.

items.title: Emergency Mobility & Evacuation Strategies
items.desc: Emergency mobility strategies are an essential component of sustainable transportation systems, as they safeguard public safety during disasters or crises. Emergency mobility goes beyond evacuation; it also ensures continued access to basic needs. In the context of SDG 2 (Zero Hunger), such strategies guarantee the distribution of emergency food supplies to affected areas, preventing communities from being cut off from food even when main routes are disrupted. Moreover, these strategies support SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by strengthening community protection through planned evacuation routes, readiness of public transport, and integration with urban spatial planning—helping communities become more resilient to extreme conditions. Emergency mobility also ensures that essential services such as healthcare and education remain accessible. Furthermore, the success of these strategies depends on cross-sector and institutional coordination, in line with SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals), thereby creating a mobility system that is responsive, inclusive, and effective in facing disasters.

items.title: Road Safety & Forensic Accident Analysis
items.desc: Improving road safety through forensic accident analysis is a crucial evidence-based approach to reducing traffic accidents and enhancing protection for road users. Forensic analysis allows for more accurate identification of accident causes, including vehicle technical aspects, road conditions, and driver behavior. The results of this study can inform more targeted policies, such as improving road design, enhancing vehicle safety standards, and implementing public education programs. This approach aligns with SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-being) as it directly contributes to reducing accident deaths and injuries, and supports SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by creating a safe and inclusive transportation system. Furthermore, implementing data-driven policies strengthens SDG 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions) through transparency and accountability in decision-making. Therefore, road safety and forensic accident analysis are not merely technical measures but also sustainable development strategies that balance safety, justice, and sustainable mobility.

items.title: Inclusive & Accessible Public Transport
items.desc: Inclusive public transportation for vulnerable groups is a key element in achieving sustainable mobility systems. With universal design that considers the needs of persons with disabilities, the elderly, children, and low-income communities, public transport expands access to education services. This aligns with SDG 4 (Quality Education), as safe and affordable transport enables students from diverse backgrounds to reach schools and universities without barriers. Moreover, inclusive public transportation supports SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by creating livable cities where all residents can actively participate in social, economic, and cultural life. Furthermore, the success of inclusive public transport depends on collaboration among governments, academia, the private sector, and communities, thereby strengthening SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals). Such collaboration ensures that policies, funding, and innovations in transportation truly address the needs of vulnerable groups, while building fairer and more sustainable cities.

items.title: Equitable Mobility in Rural & Remote Areas
items.desc: Providing fair and affordable transportation services for remote and rural areas is a strategic step to ensure inclusive and sustainable mobility. Adequate transport access in rural and remote regions not only opens economic opportunities through the distribution of agricultural products and access to markets, but also improves quality of life by facilitating access to education, healthcare, and social services. This approach aligns with SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities) by narrowing the gap between urban and rural areas, and supports SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by expanding the coverage of safe and affordable transport services. In addition, equitable mobility contributes to SDG 1 (No Poverty) and SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth) by opening access to job opportunities and enhancing community productivity. Thus, equitable mobility in remote areas is not merely about transportation, but also serves as a sustainable development instrument that strengthens social justice, economic equity, and community inclusion.

items.title: Green & Low-Carbon Vehicle Innovations
items.desc: Eco-friendly vehicle innovation is one of the key pillars in realizing sustainable mobility. The development of electric, hydrogen, and hybrid vehicles not only introduces more efficient transportation technologies, but also drives the growth of a clean energy-based automotive industry, thereby supporting SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure) through strengthened innovation and green infrastructure. At the same time, the adoption of low-emission vehicles directly contributes to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by creating healthier, more sustainable, and livable cities, as air quality improves and pollution decreases. Furthermore, the success of the transition toward eco-friendly vehicles depends on cross-country collaboration in research, investment, and clean energy policies, thus reinforcing SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals). Through global cooperation, eco-friendly vehicle innovation can become a driving force for transforming mobility toward a resilient, inclusive, and equitable future.

items.title: Mobility Planning for Air Quality & Public Health
items.desc: Transportation planning oriented toward improving air quality and public health is a strategic step to create sustainable mobility while safeguarding community well-being. By integrating vehicle emission data, travel patterns, and traffic control policies, such planning can reduce air pollution—one of the major risk factors for health. This approach aligns with SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-being) by contributing to the reduction of respiratory and cardiovascular diseases caused by pollution, and supports SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by fostering healthier, safer, and more livable cities. In addition, environmentally friendly transport policies reinforce SDG 13 (Climate Action) through the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions from the transport sector. Thus, holistically planned mobility not only enhances the efficiency of transportation systems, but also serves as a vital instrument for preserving air quality and public health in a sustainable way.

items.title: Sustainable Transport as a Driver of Smart Cities
items.desc: Sustainable transportation is a key driver in realizing smart cities that are efficient, inclusive, and environmentally friendly. By integrating smart technologies, clean energy, and connected mobility systems, sustainable transportation strengthens SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure) through the development of innovative infrastructure that supports industrial growth and accelerates the adoption of new technologies. At the same time, the implementation of sustainable transportation contributes to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by creating more efficient, healthier, and livable cities, where public mobility, pedestrian pathways, and low-emission transport systems are integral to urban planning. Furthermore, the success of smart cities based on sustainable transportation depends on SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals), as policy integration requires cross-sector collaboration among governments, academia, the private sector, and communities to ensure mobility solutions are truly inclusive and resilient.

items.title: Climate Adaptation Strategies for Transport Infrastructure
items.desc: Climate adaptation strategies for transport infrastructure are essential to ensure that mobility systems continue to function under extreme conditions such as floods, heatwaves, or rising sea levels. By integrating climate risk analysis into planning, design, and maintenance, infrastructure can be built to be more resilient and flexible in facing climate uncertainties. This approach supports SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) through adaptive construction technology innovations, while also strengthening SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by ensuring that urban and rural connectivity is maintained during disasters. In addition, these strategies contribute to SDG 13 (Climate Action) by embedding climate mitigation and adaptation into transport policies, thereby reducing the vulnerability of mobility systems to climate impacts. Thus, climate adaptation is not merely a technical measure but also a commitment to sustainable development, safeguarding social, economic, and environmental resilience in disaster-prone regions.

items.title: Freight & Logistics Innovations for Sustainable Supply Chains
items.desc: Logistics innovation is a key factor in strengthening sustainable transportation and distribution systems. From the perspective of SDG 1 (No Poverty), logistics innovation helps reduce distribution costs, making essential goods more affordable, opening market access for MSMEs and small farmers, and creating new jobs in the transport and technology sectors. Through the application of digital technology, automation, and data analytics, food supply chains can be managed more efficiently and sustainably. This supports SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) by ensuring equitable food availability, even in remote areas. In relation to SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure), logistics innovation drives industrial efficiency by lowering operational costs, increasing productivity, and fostering the development of transport infrastructure that is more resilient and adaptive to change. Furthermore, the success of logistics innovation relies on SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals), as global collaboration among governments, private sectors, academia, and international institutions is essential to build distribution systems that are transparent, inclusive, and competitive.

items.title: Rail Electrification & Smart Railway Systems
items.desc: Rail electrification is a crucial step in the transformation of sustainable transportation. By shifting from fossil fuels to electric energy, this system promotes SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) through the development of modern, environmentally friendly transportation technologies and strengthening mass mobility infrastructure. Furthermore, rail electrification supports SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by delivering more efficient, low-emission, and integrated urban mobility, resulting in improved air quality and more livable cities. Furthermore, the success of rail electrification depends on collaboration across regions and countries, in line with SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals). Integration of regional networks enables smoother connectivity between cities and countries, strengthening trade, tourism, and socio-economic collaboration. Thus, rail electrification is not only a technical innovation, but also a catalyst for transformation towards inclusive and resilient sustainable mobility.

items.title: Sustainable & Climate-Resilient Maritime Infrastructure
items.desc: Sustainable ports play a vital role in supporting sustainable development across various sectors. In the context of SDG 2 (Zero Hunger), efficient and environmentally friendly ports ensure smooth cross-regional food distribution, allowing food supplies to reach remote areas and other countries more quickly and safely. In terms of SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure), the development of modern, climate-adaptive, and digital-technology-based maritime infrastructure strengthens the logistics industry while increasing global competitiveness. Furthermore, sustainable ports also support SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals) because their operations require international cooperation between governments, the private sector, and global institutions. This collaboration creates a more transparent, efficient, and equitable trade and distribution system. Thus, sustainable ports are not only logistics hubs but also important catalysts for food security, industrial innovation, and sustainable global partnerships.

items.title: Digitalization & Smart Maritime Logistics
items.desc: Digital transformation in maritime logistics is a crucial catalyst for a more efficient and sustainable global distribution system. With the application of technologies such as the Internet of Things (IoT), big data, and real-time tracking systems, cross-border food distribution can be faster, more accurate, and more transparent, thus supporting SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) by maintaining global food security. From the perspective of SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure), digitalization drives innovation in the maritime industry through the automation of loading and unloading processes, optimization of shipping routes, and the development of smart port infrastructure that enhances global competitiveness. Furthermore, this digital transformation strengthens SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals) by creating supply chain transparency, enabling international cooperation between governments, the private sector, and global institutions to be more effective and accountable. Thus, the digitalization of maritime logistics is not merely a technical modernization, but a global strategy for food security, industrial efficiency, and sustainable partnerships.

items.title: Resilient & Sustainable Road Pavement Systems
items.desc: Resilient road pavements are a crucial foundation for sustainable mobility. With construction that can withstand heavy traffic loads and extreme climate conditions, strong roads ensure the smooth distribution of food, thus supporting SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) by maintaining a stable supply chain. In terms of SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure), the application of innovative materials and environmentally friendly technologies in road construction and maintenance promotes industrial efficiency while strengthening transportation infrastructure. Furthermore, resilient road pavements contribute to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by creating more resilient cities, where people's access to basic services is maintained even in the face of disasters or climate change. Thus, road pavement innovation is not only a technical solution, but also a strategy for food security, industrial strengthening, and sustainable urban development.

items.title: Sustainable & Climate-Resilient Air Transport
items.desc: Sustainable air transport plays a strategic role in supporting cross-sectoral development. In the context of SDG 2 (Zero Hunger), sustainable aviation enables the rapid distribution of emergency food supplies to remote or disaster-affected areas, ensuring that communities maintain access to basic needs. From the perspective of SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure), innovations in aviation—such as the use of green fuels, energy-efficient aircraft, and environmentally friendly airports—strengthen the transport industry while advancing climate-friendly technologies. Sustainable air transport also contributes to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by introducing smart air mobility integrated with urban transport systems, thereby improving connectivity and enhancing the efficiency of urban mobility. Furthermore, these achievements are only possible through SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals), as global cooperation in research, investment, regulation, and the implementation of sustainable aviation technologies is essential.

items.title: Hydrological Risk Assessment for Sustainable Mobility
items.desc: Hydrological risk assessment for sustainable mobility is an essential approach to ensuring that transportation systems remain safe, efficient, and resilient against hydrological hazards such as floods, waterlogging, erosion, and sea-level rise. By utilizing hydrological, topographical, and climatic data, transport planning can identify vulnerable points, design evacuation routes, and establish priorities for infrastructure maintenance. This strategy aligns with SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) through the development of infrastructure that is adaptive to hydrological risks, while also supporting SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) by maintaining connectivity between urban and rural areas even during water-related disasters. Furthermore, the integration of hydrological risk analysis into transport policies contributes to SDG 13 (Climate Action) by strengthening adaptive capacity to the impacts of climate change.

authorGuidelineContent.paperTitle: PAPER WRITING INSTRUCTIONS FOR FSTPT XXIX NATIONAL SYMPOSIUM,
authorGuidelineContent.author1Role: Master Student of Civil Engineering, Faculty of Engineering
authorGuidelineContent.abstractContent: This document consists of detailed paper writing instructions for Seminar Nasional FSTPT XXIX at Andalas University Padang. To submit a paper in this seminar, please follow the following guidelines for writing the paper. The author should read through these instructions before writing a paper. Papers that do not follow these instructions are not included in the seminar. The deadline for submission a draft of full paper is September 25, 2023. The paper will be reviewed by a reviewer team and the results will be informed through the author's email address. All accepted papers will be published in a seminar proceeding that has an ISBN and uploaded online. The best papers and presentations in the seminar will be awarded by the committee. The abstract is written in a concise and straightforward sentence and must not more than 250 words.
authorGuidelineContent.keywords: Keywords: keyword 1, keyword 2, keyword 3, keyword 4, keyword 5
authorGuidelineContent.abstrakContent: This document consists of detailed instructions for paper writing for ACE Conference 2023 at Andalas University Padang. To submit a paper in this seminar, please follow the writing instructions below. Authors must read all these instructions before writing a paper. Papers that do not follow these instructions will not be included in the seminar. The deadline for submission of a complete draft paper is September 25, 2023. Papers will be reviewed by a reviewer team and results will be informed through the author's email address. All accepted papers will be published in seminar proceedings with an ISBN and uploaded online. The best papers and presentations will be awarded by the committee. The abstract should be written in concise and straightforward sentences of no more than 250 words.
authorGuidelineContent.abstrakKeywords: Keywords: keyword 1, keyword 2, keyword 3, keyword 4, keyword 5
authorGuidelineContent.section1P1: These instructions are made as a guide for preparing papers suitable for presentation in the seminar. The first author is the researcher, master's or doctoral student, while subsequent authors can be supervisors or co-supervisors or colleagues in the research group.
authorGuidelineContent.section1P2: Authors must write their papers according to these instructions so that the paper can be accepted. The submitted paper must be written using
authorGuidelineContent.section2_1Intro: The paper must follow the following format:
authorGuidelineContent.format2: Margin: Top 3 cm, Bottom 4 cm, Left 3 cm, and Right 3 cm
authorGuidelineContent.format3: Font type is Times New Roman
authorGuidelineContent.format5: Text writing: single spaced and single column text
authorGuidelineContent.format6: Writing in headers and footers is prohibited
authorGuidelineContent.formatNote: * Please recheck for any typos and spelling errors. Follow the format settings using the Ms-Word styles provided in the template.
authorGuidelineContent.section2_2Intro: In this paper file, Ms-Word styles have been provided for formatting the paper. Authors simply need to click the corresponding style to get proper paper formatting.
authorGuidelineContent.styleTitleDesc: Font size Times New Roman 16pt, bold, single spaced.
authorGuidelineContent.styleAuthorDesc: Author names written in one line, add superscript numbers for affiliation identification.
authorGuidelineContent.styleAffiliationDesc: Department/Study Program, Faculty, University, and Email information.
authorGuidelineContent.styleAbstractDesc: Abstract – header for title and Abstract for content (single column, single paragraph).
authorGuidelineContent.styleKeyword: Provide a maximum of five keywords using the style
authorGuidelineContent.styleKeywordAfter: . Bold the word 'Abstract:' before the keywords.
authorGuidelineContent.styleH3After: . Chapter numbers will be automatically generated by Ms-Word.
authorGuidelineContent.section2_3P1: Equations, tables, and figures must be placed between the text at the appropriate location for discussion of the table and figure. Tables must be numbered sequentially. Table titles are placed above the table.
authorGuidelineContent.tableCaption: Table 1. Service conditions of travel attributes
authorGuidelineContent.section2_3P2: Figures must be numbered sequentially. Figure titles are placed below the figure. Figures must be clear and easy to read. See Figure 1, equations must also be numbered sequentially. Numbers are placed on the right side of the equation.
authorGuidelineContent.figureCaption: Figure 1. Paper selection diagram at ACE 8
authorGuidelineContent.section3Intro: The paper structure should consist of the following main components:
authorGuidelineContent.section4P1: The bibliography should only contain references cited in the paper text. This bibliography is written in ascending order based on the last name of the author. Use the style
authorGuidelineContent.section4Format: Reference writing format follows the APA 6th method, the example of writing is as follows: Author name. Year of publication. Publication title. Place of publication: publisher.
authorGuidelineContent.section5P1: If needed, add acknowledgments to parties who helped in the research, paper writing, or seminar execution.
authorGuidelineContent.section6P1: The paper can be supplemented with appendices containing tables, figures, maps, and others if necessary to be attached. Appendices must comply with language requirements, page numbering, header, page format, table and figure format.
